item.title: 3.2 Focus on Assumptions to Effectively Weaken an Argument.
item.description: Lesson Content As we’ve discussed, an argument is composed of a conclusion, the evidence that supports the conclusion, any background information, and the assumptions that connect the evidence with the conclusion. Conclusion Evidence Background Assumptions On the GMAT, we’ll never find an answer choice that weakens an argument by attacking the premises (that is, the evidence) upon which the conclusion rests, because while in real life premises may turn out to be invalid, on the GMAT the premises are taken to be true.

item.title: 3.3 Spotting a Weaken the Argument Question
item.description: Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Weaken the Argument question is easy, because these questions utilize predictable language. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the conclusion drawn above? Which of the following, if true, does the most to call into question the claim made? Which of the following, if discovered, would be evidence against the speculation above?

item.title: 3.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Weaken the Argument questions are the most common type of Critical Reasoning questions on the GMAT. The essence of a Weaken the Argument question is simple; it asks us to select an answer option that decreases the persuasiveness of an argument. 💡The essence of a Weaken the Argument question is simple; it asks us to select an answer option that decreases the persuasiveness of an argument. In other words, it asks us to weaken an argument.

item.title: 3.8 Additive Phrases Don’t Create Plural Subjects
item.description: Lesson Content In the last section, we saw that the use of the word “and” can result in the creation of a compound plural subject that requires a plural verb. In this section, we’re going to discuss additive phrases. What we’ll see as we discuss additive phrases is that, even though expressions such as “along with” or “as well as” resemble “and,” additive phrases don’t work the way “and” works with sentence subjects.

item.title: SC-4 Modifiers
item.description: A modifier describes/provides extra information about something else in the sentence. Incorrectly used modifiers lead to ambiguity or illogical meaning. 💡 Many modifiers answer the questions who, what, when, where, or why Adjectives & Adverbs These are one-word modifiers. Adjective → ONLY Noun/Pronoun Adverb → Almost anything EXCEPT Noun/Pronoun Verbs, Adjectives Prepositional Phrases Entire Clauses Many adverbs are formed by adding “-ly” to the adjective 💡 Often Adjective ↔ Adverb use will be interchanged as the sentence error.

item.title: SC-3 Sentence Structure
item.description: Every sentence must have a noun (subject) that performs the action (verb). Subject-Verb Errors Subject/Verb Missing A sentence missing the subject/verb is just a fragment. In GMAT, the correct answer must be a complete sentence or independent clause. E.g: The cat sitting by the stairs. ❌ 🚨 Verbs ≠ Complete Sentence Here although sitting looks like a verb, its a working verb that can run a sentence by itself. The cat sitting by the stairs WATCHED the mouse ✅ | watched is a working verb here.

item.title: SC-2 Grammar and Meaning
item.description: Must-Know Grammar Terms Clause Set of words containing a subject and a working verb Eg: She applied for the job. She- subject & applied- working verb 💡 Using the question “Who did what?” will point to the subject and working verb Independent Clause: Standalone correct sentence. Dependent Clause: Also contains a verb. But not standalone in a sentence. These are your sentence fragments. Modifier Provides additional information in a sentence. Adjectives are a modifier.

item.title: SC-1 Question Format and Solution Process
item.description: Question Format Although William Pereira first gained national recognition for his movie set designs, including those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remember him as the architect of the Transamerica Tower, the Malibu campus of Pepperdine University, and the city of Irvine. (A) including those for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations remember (B) like that for Reap the Wild Wind and Jane Eyre, future generations will remember

item.title: Extreme Value Theory (EVT)
item.description: Real-life data has fatter tails, that is a very high probability of shorter losses. In EVT, we fit the distribution to only the tail of the data. It’s also called the semi-parametric method. Applicable only at the tails and for random variables >0 💡 so we multiply the -ve returns with (-1) and fit it. Method We have to fit a model to the tail of the dataset beyond a cutoff

item.title: Value At Risk (VaR)
item.description: It’s a statistical measure of potential loss. This is the amount we should be prepared to cover for. VaR is the worst loss, over a target horizon, such that there is a low pre-specified probability that the actual loss will be higher. $$ P(l>VaR)\leq 1-C $$ where $P(l\leq VaR)= C$ Discrete Example of calculating VaR Gains Prob 10 Cr 98% -2 Cr 1.5% -5 Cr 0.5% $\text{Expected Gain}\ E(G) = (0.

item.title: Introduction to Financial Risk
item.description: Defining Risk Risk is the probability of a loss. Financially Risky Decision Some examples of risky decisions in finance: Investment Options: Where to invest in a financial instrument? Debt/Equity Balances: How much to invest in a financial instrument? Financial Risk Sources Sources of these risk are mostly macro-economic sources like: Business cycles leading to changing interest rates Govt. Policies (Demonetization) Technological Changes (Crypto, FinTech) Wars & Natural Calamities Risk Management Steps Identify the risk Assessing & Measuring the risk Monitoring & Mitigate the risk Using Cash/Savings Derivatives (covered in DRM) Share/Transfer(partially) risk through (re)insurance Types of Risk 1.

item.title: Forwards and Future Contracts
item.description: Futures Data.xlsx Forward Contract Contract Farming Contract farming can be defined as agricultural production carried out according to an agreement between a buyer and farmers, which establishes conditions for the production and marketing of a farm product or products. Typically, the farmer agrees to provide agreed quantities of a specific agricultural product. Why is the farmer entering into the contract? The farmer fears drop in prices they fix a price to ensure certain profits.

item.title: CR-9 Assumption
item.description: An assumption is simply an unstated premise that must be true in order for the argument to be true. It is an integral component of the argument that the author takes for granted and leaves unsaid. 💡 The answer to an assumption question must be something that the author must believe in order for the conclusion to be properly drawn. The assumptions are minimal & can’t contain extraneous statements Diff. b/w Strengthen & Assumption Questions Strengthen questions ask us to support the argument in any way possible.

item.title: CR-8 Strengthen
item.description: These questions ask you to identify the answer choice that best supports the argument. The correct answer choice does not necessarily justify the arguments, nor is the correct answer choice necessarily an assumption of the argument. 💡 The correct answer choice simply helps the argument in some way. Rules to Solve (Same as Weaken) The stimulus will always contain an argument since you are asked to weaken the author’s reasoning. Focus on the conclusion.

item.title: CR-7 Cause and Effect Reasoning
item.description: Cause & effect reasoning asserts/denies whether one thing causes another, or, is caused by another. 💡 Most tested logical concept in GMAT CR stimuli. What is Causality? Causality occurs when one event is said to make another occur. The cause is the event that makes the other occur; the effect is the event that follows from the cause. By definition, the cause must occur before the effect. Recognizing Causality Cause & Effect relationship has a signature characteristics- a single cause makes the effect happen.

item.title: CR-6 Weaken
item.description: Weaken type question require you to select the answer choice that undermines the author’s argument as decisively as possible. 💡 MOST FREQUENT CR QUESTION TYPE Rules to Solve The stimulus will always contain an argument since you are asked to weaken the author’s reasoning. Focus on the conclusion. The more we know about its specifics, the better armed we will be to deciding the correct answer choice. Information in the stimulus is suspect.

item.title: CR-5 Main Point
item.description: Main Point questions ask you to summarize the author’s point of view. Fact Test + Main Point As with Must Be True, Main point answer choice must pass the fact test. But apart from that it should also capture the author’s point. Applying the primary objectives, we’ll most likely have selected the conclusion of the stimulus already. So we have the main point with us already. Identify Primary Conclusion → Find paraphrase of conclusion in answer choices

item.title: CR-4 Must Be True
item.description: Must Be True Must by True questions require you to select an answer choice that is proven by the stimulus information. The correct answer choice maybe: Paraphrase of the stimulus Logical consequence of one or more parts of the stimulus Fact Test The correct answer choice can always be proven by referring to the facts stated in the stimulus. Any answer choice we select should not be selected on the basis that it “sounds good”.

item.title: CR-3 The Question Stem and Answer Choices
item.description: The question stem poses a question directed at the stimulus. It specifies the task you must perform in order to get credit for the problem. Question Stem Tasks Identify details of the stimulus Describe the structure of the argument Strengthen or weaken the argument Identify inferences, main points, and assumptions Recognize errors of reasoning Reconcile conflicts Find arguments that are identical in nature Analyze the Question Stem It’s important to correctly classify the question into one of the types since that’ll help us in deciding which part of the stimulus to focus on.

item.title: CR-2 The Stimulus
item.description: Steps Decide upon the broad topic Read it VERY carefully to capture all the details Identify the problems between each part of the stimulus Don’t panic if you don’t understand a terminology. Focus on understanding the how the ideas connect with each other Speaker Identifiers Pay attention to the speaker. Often it will help in identifying the broad topic addressed in the stimulus Business: Manager, Executive, Business Plan Politics: Politician, Editorial, Lawmaker, Activist Science/Medicine: Doctor, Physician, Scientist, Researcher Some identifiers are confusing or too broad.

item.title: CR-1 General Tips & Question Structure
item.description: Read all answer choices. Often the test-maker puts an attractive wrong choice before the right one Assumptions: Never assume anything blatantly Assumptions under common sense are alright. E.g. The sky is sometimes cloudy TV shows are transmitted as signals to homes Structure of a CR Question 🚨 Read the parts in same order as given in the question 🚨 Primary Objectives of CR 🚨 Determine whether the stimulus contains an argument or only a set of facts If it contains an argument identify the conclusion of the argument.

item.title: Rich Content
item.description: This sample post tests the followings: Category, sub-category nesting in the sidebar. Hero image and other images are in images folder inside this post directory. Different media rendering like image, tweet, YouTube video, Vimeo video etc. Image Sample Tweet Sample Owl bet you'll lose this staring contest 🦉 pic.twitter.com/eJh4f2zncC — San Diego Zoo Wildlife Alliance (@sandiegozoo) October 26, 2021 YouTube Video Sample Vimeo Video Sample

item.title: Shortcodes Samples
item.description: This is a sample post intended to test the followings: Default hero image. Different shortcodes. Alert The following alerts are available in this theme. This is sample alert with type="success". This is sample alert with type="danger". This is sample alert with type="warning". This is sample alert with type="info". This is sample alert with type="dark". This is sample alert with type="primary". This is sample alert with type="secondary". Image A sample image without any attribute.

item.title: Introduction
Basic Types
item.description: Strings str := "Hello" Multiline string str := `Multiline string` Numbers Typical types num := 3 // int num := 3. // float64 num := 3 + 4i // complex128 num := byte('a') // byte (alias for uint8) Other Types var u uint = 7 // uint (unsigned) var p float32 = 22.7 // 32-bit float Arrays // var numbers [5]int numbers := [...]int{0, 0, 0, 0, 0} Pointers func main () { b := *getPointer() fmt.

item.title: Flow Control
item.description: Condition if day == "sunday" || day == "saturday" { rest() } else if day == "monday" && isTired() { groan() } else { work() } if _, err := doThing(); err != nil { fmt.Println("Uh oh") Switch switch day { case "sunday": // cases don't "fall through" by default! fallthrough case "saturday": rest() default: work() } Loop for count := 0; count <= 10; count++ { fmt.Println("My counter is at", count) } entry := []string{"Jack","John","Jones"} for i, val := range entry { fmt.

item.title: File Manipulation
item.description: Condition if day == "sunday" || day == "saturday" { rest() } else if day == "monday" && isTired() { groan() } else { work() } if _, err := doThing(); err != nil { fmt.Println("Uh oh")

item.title: Bash Variables
item.description: Variable NAME="John" echo $NAME echo "$NAME" echo "${NAME} Condition if [[ -z "$string" ]]; then echo "String is empty" elif [[ -n "$string" ]]; then echo "String is not empty" fi
